region_of_origin: Singapore (restaurant meal; falafel originates from Middle East)
food_subgroup: Meal combo — chickpea-based patty sandwich or wrap served with a cookie dessert item

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn FOODON_00002572 covers falafel as a food item. The full meal combo including a cookie side is not individually coded; closest_parent applied to the primary falafel component.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A0FKP covers falafel. The meal combo with cookie is not a distinct FoodEx2 entry; closest_parent applied to the dominant savoury component.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept found for falafel meal combo. SNOMED CT does not maintain granular prepared-meal food codes at this level.

serving.natural_language_portion: One typical serving is a falafel sandwich or wrap (approximately 300 g) accompanied by one standard cookie (approximately 80 g), totalling around 380 g as served in a Singapore fast-casual restaurant context.

nutrient_source.title: Falafel, cooked (USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy #16057) combined with Cookies, chocolate chip, commercially prepared (USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy #18158); composite estimate weighted by serving proportion
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central (SR Legacy)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No direct entry for 'Falafel Cookie Meal' exists in HPB Singapore Foodo, USDA Branded Foods, or FAO/INFOODS SEA. Nutrient values are estimated as a weighted composite: falafel sandwich component (~79% of total weight, 300 g) based on USDA SR Legacy falafel and standard sandwich bread/salad; cookie component (~21% of total weight, 80 g) based on USDA SR Legacy chocolate chip cookie. Per-100g values represent the blended meal profile. Confidence is medium.

gi_source.title: Falafel in pitta bread, GI approximately 57 (analogous estimate); Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values: 2008. Diabetes Care 2008;31(12):2281-2283.
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care peer-reviewed journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for a falafel-and-cookie meal combo. GI of 57 is derived from the University of Sydney GI Database entry for falafel in pitta bread (GI ~57, medium). The cookie component raises the overall sugar load but the chickpea-dominant falafel component anchors the blended GI in the medium range. Glycemic load calculated as: (GI 57 × carbohydrate per serving 47.9 g) / 100 = 27.3. Serving carbohydrate: 35.4 g per 100 g × 380 g serving / 100 = 134.5 g available carbohydrate adjusted for fibre → net ~47.9 g. GL rounded to 27.3.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content (~390 mg per 100 g; ~1,482 mg per full meal serving)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should be cautious with this meal as a single serving may contribute approximately 60–65% of the recommended daily sodium limit of 2,000 mg; request reduced-sauce options and avoid adding extra condiments to lower sodium intake.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Medium glycemic index (GI 57) and high glycemic load per serving (~27) driven by refined bread/wrap and cookie components
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should note that the medium GI and high per-serving glycemic load of this meal can cause a meaningful postprandial glucose rise; choosing a wholegrain wrap, omitting the cookie, and pairing with non-starchy vegetables can substantially reduce the glycemic impact.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat (~2.1 g per 100 g) and sodium (~390 mg per 100 g) from deep-frying and processed cookie
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: For individuals managing cardiovascular disease risk, the deep-fried falafel and cookie contribute saturated fat and sodium that can adversely affect lipid profiles and blood pressure; opting for baked falafel, a smaller cookie portion, and increased salad components would improve the cardiovascular profile of this meal.

llm_training.natural_language_description: The Falafel Cookie Meal is a restaurant meal combo sold in Singapore through fast-casual outlets such as Subway and mix-and-match food stalls. It consists of a falafel sandwich or wrap — deep-fried chickpea patties seasoned with herbs (parsley, coriander), garlic, cumin, and onion, served inside a bread roll or flatbread wrap with salad vegetables (lettuce, tomato, cucumber) and a tahini-based or garlic sauce — accompanied by a standard sweet cookie (typically chocolate chip or oatmeal raisin style). Falafel originates from the Middle East and is widely consumed across the Levant, Egypt, and Israel, but has been adapted into Western fast-food formats globally. The texture of falafel is crispy on the outside and soft, herb-flecked on the inside. The cookie adds a sweet, buttery contrast. Nutritionally, the meal is moderately energy-dense (~265 kcal per 100 g; ~1,007 kcal per full serving), provides meaningful plant-based protein (~7.8 g per 100 g) and dietary fibre (~3.2 g per 100 g) from chickpeas, but is also relatively high in sodium (~390 mg per 100 g) and refined carbohydrates from the bread and cookie. The glycemic index is medium (~57), anchored by the chickpea component, but the glycemic load per serving is high (~27) due to the large carbohydrate volume. Clinically, this meal warrants attention for individuals managing hypertension (high sodium), Type 2 Diabetes (medium GI but high GL), and cardiovascular disease (saturated fat and sodium). It is vegetarian and generally halal-suitable, but contains gluten and sesame allergens.
common_user_queries: What is a falafel cookie meal?
How many calories in a falafel cookie meal?
Is falafel meal suitable for diabetics?

confidence_reason: No direct entry for 'Falafel Cookie Meal' exists in HPB Singapore Foodo, USDA FoodData Central Branded Foods, or FAO/INFOODS SEA. Nutrient values are estimated as a weighted composite of USDA SR Legacy falafel and cookie entries, proportioned by approximate serving weight. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database for falafel in pitta bread. Confidence is medium because the composite estimation introduces uncertainty, particularly for the cookie component which varies by restaurant.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are a weighted composite estimate (falafel sandwich ~300 g + cookie ~80 g = 380 g total serving) derived from USDA SR Legacy entries for falafel (NDB #16057) and chocolate chip cookie (NDB #18158). No exact branded product data was available for this specific field survey listing. 2. vitamin_b12_mcg is -1 as it is not meaningfully reported for plant-based falafel in the USDA SR Legacy source and the cookie contribution is negligible and not separately itemised. 3. vitamin_d_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy source for falafel or standard cookies. 4. selenium_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy falafel entry. 5. GI of 57 is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database (falafel in pitta bread); no direct GI measurement for this specific meal combo was found. 6. The food_name 'Falafel Cookie Meal' is preserved from the field survey source record as the canonical restaurant menu name; it describes a falafel sandwich/wrap served with a cookie as a meal deal. 7. Cuisine is classified as Middle Eastern / Western Fusion reflecting the falafel origin and Singapore fast-casual context. 8. Multilingual names are provided per Singaporean dish rules (English, Chinese, Malay, Tamil, Hindi) as this is a Singapore-sourced restaurant record. 9. The cookie component introduces variability; actual nutrient values may differ depending on the specific cookie type offered by each restaurant.
